agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand levels. The CEO's description is intentionally high-level and leaves significant implementation details unspecified — typical of an early-stage product vision communicated at an all-hands meeting, not a formal specification.",
    "bounded_context": "Pricing",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "DynaPricing", "definition": "The dynamic pricing product/system that adjusts delivery prices in real time based on demand signals" },
      { "term": "Demand", "definition": "A measure of delivery request volume relative to available capacity in a given market/time window — exact definition unspecified" },
      { "term": "Guardrails", "definition": "Upper and/or lower bounds constraining how far automated pricing can deviate from a baseline, preventing extreme or irrational prices" },
      { "term": "Real-time adjustment", "definition": "Price changes that are computed and applied continuously or near-continuously, not via batch/manual processes" },
      { "term": "Delivery price", "definition": "The fee charged to the customer for a delivery, which DynaPricing dynamically sets" }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must increase when demand is high relative to baseline/capacity",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is the core pricing policy — the direction of the correlation (high demand → higher price) is a business strategy decision, not a system integrity constraint. A VP could decide to cap surge pricing or disable it for loyalty customers.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand exceeds a defined 'high' threshold, the computed price must be greater than the baseline price for the same delivery parameters"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must decrease when demand is low relative to baseline/capacity",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a competitive pricing policy to attract volume during low-demand periods. Management could choose to hold prices at baseline instead of discounting.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand falls below a defined 'low' threshold, the computed price must be less than the baseline price for the same delivery parameters"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must occur in real time — the system must not rely on manual intervention or batch processing to update prices",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an operational requirement, not a data integrity constraint. A fallback to manual pricing during an outage would be acceptable — the system would not be corrupt, just degraded.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a change in demand level, the system must compute and make available a new price within a defined SLA window (exact latency TBD)"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "The pricing system must be fully automated — no human approval is required for individual price changes within guardrail bounds",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is an operational policy. Management could reintroduce manual approval gates for certain markets or price tiers without corrupting system state.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand changes and the resulting price falls within guardrail bounds, the price is published without requiring human approval"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Price guardrails must exist to constrain the range of automated price adjustments, preventing prices from reaching extreme or irrational values",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Without guardrails, an algorithmic error or demand spike could produce a $10,000 delivery fee or a $0.00 fee — either of which would constitute a system integrity failure. A price floor below cost destroys margin; a price ceiling breach destroys customer trust. The existence of bounds is a hard safety constraint even if the specific bound values are policy.",
      "testable_assertion": "No computed delivery price may be published outside the configured minimum and maximum price bounds"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "The price floor guardrail must ensure the delivery price never falls below a minimum that preserves profitability (i.e., price >= cost floor)",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Selling a delivery below cost is a financial integrity violation — the system would be actively destroying value. Even aggressive discounting has a floor at marginal cost. This is a physical/financial constraint, not a policy preference.",
      "testable_assertion": "No computed delivery price may be less than the configured minimum price floor, and that floor must be >= 0"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The price ceiling guardrail must ensure the delivery price never exceeds a configured maximum, protecting against runaway surge pricing",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "An unbounded price ceiling exposes the company to reputational damage, potential regulatory action (price gouging), and customer churn. The ceiling value is policy, but its enforcement is a system integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "No computed delivery price may exceed the configured maximum price ceiling"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "A delivery price must always be a positive monetary value greater than zero",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A zero or negative delivery price is nonsensical in the logistics domain — it would mean paying customers to take deliveries. This is a fundamental domain constraint on what a 'price' is.",
      "testable_assertion": "Every computed delivery price must be > 0"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-9",
      "rule": "The pricing relationship must be monotonic with respect to demand: higher demand must never produce a lower price than lower demand, all else being equal",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a logical consistency policy derived from the CEO's stated intent. However, it is conceivable that business rules could create non-monotonic pricing (e.g., loyalty overrides, promotional caps), so it is a policy, not a hard invariant.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given two demand levels D1 > D2 with all other parameters equal, price(D1) >= price(D2)"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-10",
      "rule": "The system must balance competitiveness with profitability — prices should not be so high as to lose all customers, nor so low as to be unprofitable",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a strategic business objective, not a concrete enforceable rule. The tension between competitiveness and profitability is resolved by policy decisions (guardrail values, pricing curves), not by system invariants.",
      "testable_assertion": "NOT DIRECTLY TESTABLE — this is a strategic goal that manifests through BR-5, BR-6, BR-7, and margin/conversion KPIs"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "Current demand is above the high-demand threshold for a delivery zone",
      "when": "A customer requests a delivery price quote",
      "then": "The quoted price is higher than the baseline price for that zone and delivery parameters"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "Current demand is below the low-demand threshold for a delivery zone",
      "when": "A customer requests a delivery price quote",
      "then": "The quoted price is lower than the baseline price for that zone and delivery parameters"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "The pricing engine computes a price that exceeds the configured maximum ceiling",
      "when": "The price is about to be published or quoted",
      "then": "The price is clamped to the maximum ceiling value and the clamping event is logged"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "The pricing engine computes a price that falls below the configured minimum floor",
      "when": "The price is about to be published or quoted",
      "then": "The price is clamped to the minimum floor value and the clamping event is logged"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "Demand conditions change in a delivery zone",
      "when": "The system detects the change",
      "then": "A new price is computed and made available without human intervention, within the defined latency SLA"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "Any combination of demand inputs",
      "when": "The pricing engine computes a delivery price",
      "then": "The resulting price is always a positive monetary value greater than zero"
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Demand signal is unavailable or stale (e.g., data pipeline failure)", "expected_behavior": "System must fall back to a safe default price (e.g., baseline price) rather than computing a price from missing data. The outage must be surfaced via alerting." },
    { "scenario": "Demand spikes instantaneously to extreme levels (e.g., 100x normal)", "expected_behavior": "Price ceiling guardrail clamps the output. The system must not crash, overflow, or produce an unbounded price." },
    { "scenario": "Demand drops to zero (no requests in a zone)", "expected_behavior": "Price floor guardrail ensures the price does not fall below cost. Zero demand does not mean zero price." },
    { "scenario": "Multiple rapid demand fluctuations cause price oscillation (flapping)", "expected_behavior": "Pricing should incorporate smoothing or dampening to avoid confusing customers with wildly changing prices within seconds. Exact mechanism is TBD but the risk is real." },
    { "scenario": "A customer sees one price, but demand changes before they confirm the order", "expected_behavior": "The business must decide on a price-lock window (quote validity period). This is unspecified in the CEO's description and is a critical gap." },
    { "scenario": "Guardrail configuration is missing or invalid (e.g., floor > ceiling)", "expected_behavior": "System must refuse to start or refuse to quote prices rather than operating without guardrails. Fail closed, not open." },
    { "scenario": "Two adjacent delivery zones have vastly different demand levels", "expected_behavior": "Customers may game the system by selecting pickup/dropoff points just across a zone boundary. The pricing model needs to account for zone boundary arbitrage." },
    { "scenario": "Regulatory price-gouging laws apply during emergencies or natural disasters", "expected_behavior": "The system must support an emergency override mode that caps prices or disables surge pricing entirely. This is unmentioned but legally critical in many jurisdictions." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Dynamic pricing directly affects revenue and margin. Upside: captures surplus during high demand, increases volume during low demand. Downside: incorrect guardrails could destroy margin (floor too low) or revenue (ceiling too low). Pricing errors at scale compound rapidly — a 5% pricing bug on 100K daily deliveries is catastrophic.",
    "operational": "Fully automated pricing removes manual pricing workflows but introduces new operational concerns: monitoring pricing engine health, guardrail configuration management, demand signal pipeline reliability, and incident response for pricing anomalies.",
    "user": "Customers will experience variable pricing, which can erode trust if not communicated transparently. Surge pricing is a known source of customer dissatisfaction (see: Uber backlash). Clear communication of why prices vary and what the maximum can be is essential for adoption.",
    "risk": "HIGH — Regulatory risk from price-gouging laws in certain jurisdictions during emergencies. Reputational risk from perceived unfair pricing. Financial risk from algorithmic errors producing incorrect prices at scale. Competitive risk if competitors offer fixed/transparent pricing.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Will pay variable prices — sometimes more, sometimes less than today's fixed/manual pricing. Requires transparency to maintain trust." },
      { "group": "Finance/Revenue team", "impact": "Gains a powerful lever for margin optimization but must define and maintain guardrail parameters. Needs dashboards to monitor pricing health." },
      { "group": "Operations/Dispatch", "impact": "Demand signals they produce become inputs to pricing — data quality and latency requirements increase." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Will receive complaints about price variations. Needs training, scripts, and tools to explain dynamic pricing to customers." },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance", "impact": "Must review pricing for regulatory compliance, especially anti-price-gouging laws and consumer protection regulations. This is NOT mentioned in the CEO's description." },
      { "group": "Sales/Partnerships", "impact": "Enterprise/contract customers may need fixed-rate agreements that bypass or cap dynamic pricing. DynaPricing must support exemptions." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 30,
    "reasoning": "The CEO's description is a high-level vision statement from an all-hands meeting, not a product specification. It contains no concrete definitions of 'high demand' vs 'low demand,' no guardrail values, no latency requirements, no zone/market segmentation, no price-lock semantics, no regulatory considerations, and uses hedging language ('probably,' 'don't go crazy'). Almost every critical parameter is undefined. The rules I extracted are directionally correct but cannot be implemented without substantial specification work.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Price goes up with high demand, down with low demand (directional intent is clear)",
      "System must be automated (explicitly stated)",
      "Guardrails must exist to bound prices (explicitly stated, though vaguely)"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Definition of 'high demand' and 'low demand' — no thresholds, no metric definition",
      "Guardrail values — 'don't go crazy' is not a specification",
      "Price-lock/quote validity semantics — completely unaddressed",
      "Zone/market segmentation — is pricing per-zone, per-city, per-route?",
      "Demand signal definition — order volume? capacity utilization? courier availability?",
      "Real-time latency SLA — 'real time' could mean 100ms or 10 minutes",
      "Regulatory compliance — zero mention of price-gouging laws",
      "Contract/enterprise customer exemptions — not mentioned",
      "Price smoothing/dampening — not mentioned but operationally critical"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "A 'baseline price' exists that serves as the anchor for dynamic adjustments",
      "Demand is measurable and the company has or will build a demand signal pipeline",
      "Guardrails include both a floor and a ceiling",
      "The price floor is at or above the cost of fulfilling the delivery",
      "Prices are per-delivery, not subscription or flat-rate",
      "The system operates within legal/regulatory frameworks even though none were mentioned"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The definition of 'demand' is completely unspecified. Is it order volume per hour? Ratio of orders to available couriers? Geographic density of requests? The entire pricing model depends on this definition and it is absent.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering", "Finance", "Operations"],
      "options": ["Define demand as order volume per zone per time window", "Define demand as order-to-courier ratio (capacity utilization)", "Define demand as a composite signal incorporating multiple factors"],
      "recommendation": "Define demand as order-to-courier capacity ratio, as this captures both the supply and demand sides and is the most operationally meaningful metric in logistics."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "'Probably have some guardrails' and 'prices don't go crazy' are not specifications. We need concrete guardrail definitions: What are the floor and ceiling values? Are they absolute amounts, percentages of baseline, or multiples? Who configures them? How often can they change?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Finance", "Legal/Compliance", "Customers"],
      "options": ["Fixed absolute floor/ceiling (e.g., $2 min, $50 max)", "Percentage-based bounds relative to baseline (e.g., 50%-300% of baseline)", "Multiplier-based bounds (e.g., 0.5x-3x surge multiplier)"],
      "recommendation": "Use multiplier-based bounds relative to a baseline price, as this scales naturally across different delivery types and distances. Finance must own the multiplier configuration."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No mention of price-lock or quote validity. When a customer sees a price, how long is it valid? If demand changes between quote and order confirmation, does the price change? This is a critical UX and business decision.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Engineering", "Customer Support"],
      "options": ["Price locks for a fixed window (e.g., 5 minutes)", "Price is advisory until order is confirmed (may change)", "Price locks once customer begins checkout flow"],
      "recommendation": "Lock price for a defined window (e.g., 5 minutes) once quoted. This balances revenue accuracy with customer trust. Airline and rideshare industry precedent supports this approach."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No mention of regulatory compliance. Many jurisdictions have anti-price-gouging laws that prohibit excessive price increases during emergencies or disasters. A dynamic pricing system in logistics MUST account for this.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "Customers", "Executive team"],
      "options": ["Build an emergency override that disables surge pricing", "Cap surge multiplier at a legally safe level globally", "Implement jurisdiction-specific pricing rules"],
      "recommendation": "Build an emergency override mode that can instantly cap or disable surge pricing per-market. Legal review of all operating jurisdictions is required before launch."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No mention of how DynaPricing interacts with contract/enterprise customers who may have negotiated fixed rates. Dynamic pricing could violate existing contractual obligations.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Sales/Partnerships", "Enterprise customers", "Legal"],
      "options": ["Exempt contract customers from dynamic pricing entirely", "Apply dynamic pricing but cap at contractual rate", "Renegotiate contracts to allow dynamic pricing within bounds"],
      "recommendation": "Exempt contract customers by default and ensure the pricing engine supports customer-tier-based overrides."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
